problem_statement: An internal media archive intermittently times out while uploading thumbnails to an object service. Failures cluster on one Kubernetes node pool after a networking rollout, but the object-service client, pod image, request data, and application settings are nominally identical. Diagnose the earliest supported failure without treating downstream retries as the cause.

observations.statement: Upload probe up-31 under revision media-18 succeeds from node pool maple and times out from node pool cedar with identical request bytes and pod image digest.
The same up-31 probe and media-18 image on cedar reaches the object-service endpoint but times out during body transfer.

A bounded network capture summary on cedar shows repeated retransmission beginning at encapsulated frames above 1380 bytes and no returned fragmentation-needed signal. Maple carries the same frames without retransmission.
Cedar nodes report overlay MTU 1450 while their new underlay path supports 1400; maple reports overlay MTU 1350. The mismatch first appears in the cedar CNI rollout inventory.
In an isolated network namespace mirroring cedar, lowering only the pod-side MTU to 1350 makes 100 of 100 up-31 transfers succeed; restoring 1450 reproduces 100 of 100 timeouts.
The object service returns healthy latency for small requests from both pools and for large requests from maple; service-side logs show no rejection and no saturation during cedar failures.
Rendered client timeout, retry count, endpoint, credentials, and proxy settings match across pools. Increasing only the timeout delays the same failure and raises retry volume.
Application traces on both pools invoke the same upload path and emit identical content lengths and checksums before the socket write; no media-18 source diff overlaps transfer framing.
